summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Follow-up of chronic problems and management of depression and hypertension.

History of Present Illness
Edward is a 59-year-old male with a past medical history significant for depression, hypertension, and prior rotator cuff repair. He presents for follow-up of his chronic problems. Six months ago, he was trying to think of some new strategies to manage his depression and was not wanting to go on medication because he's already on a bunch of meds. He's been out swimming at the pool a lot this summer and fall, and things have been well going with his depression. In terms of his high blood pressure, he says it's been struggling with it lately. He still has good days and bad days, and he does take his medicine daily, but he knows that burger and wine, every once in a while, sneaks in there, and that salt does… well you know what that does. He's checking his blood pressures at home with a machine at CVS pharmacy about once a week. He had his rotator cuff repaired about eight months ago and has been stretching with a yoga ball and getting stronger each time. He's progressing to exercises at home now. He reports no symptoms at all, no nasal congestion or chest pain, shortness of breath, or anything. However, he does notice a little bit of swelling in his ankles, mainly near the end of the day, which goes away by the next morning. He's been doing well overall, and he's happy to continue his current management strategies.

Findings
The patient's blood pressure is elevated today, 156 over 94. A slight three out of six systolic ejection murmur is appreciated. The doctor reports 1 to 2+ pitting edema in the legs. The patient's lab results are all stable, including his creatinine. An EKG shows no evidence of coronary artery disease. The patient's blood pressure readings have been running high over the past couple of months. The doctor reports a slight three out of six systolic ejection murmur.

Assessment
The patient has depression, hypertension, and a history of rotator cuff repair. His depression is currently well-managed with counseling and lifestyle modifications. His hypertension is not well controlled at this time. The rotator cuff repair is stable.

Plan
Increase norvasc to 10 mg a day. Order a lipid panel and an echocardiogram. Continue counseling once a week. Recommend decreasing salt intake. Follow up in four weeks if symptoms persist. Schedule a follow-up appointment in four weeks.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Follow-up of chronic problems.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Edward Butler is a 59-year-old male with a past medical history significant for depression, hypertension, and prior rotator cuff repair. He presents for a follow-up of his chronic problems.

Regarding his depression, he has been doing pretty well over the last 6 months. The patient notes that he sees a counselor once a week. He states that he has been swimming at the pool a lot this summer and fall. The patient has preferred to avoid medications to treat this.

Regarding his hypertension, he states that he has good days and bad days. He adds that he takes his Norvasc daily. The patient states that he checks his blood pressure at CVS about once weekly. He does admit to occasionally drinking wine and eating burgers.

The patient had his rotator cuff repaired about 8 months ago. He states that he is doing well. He states that he is no longer seeing a physical therapist in this center, however, he is progressing to exercises at home. The patient notes that he stretches with a yoga ball and is getting stronger.

He notes that he experiences mild swelling in his ankles, mainly near the end of the day. He states that the swelling resolves by the next morning. The patient denies nasal congestion, chest pain, or shortness of breath.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Ears, Nose, Mouth and Throat: Denies nasal congestion.
• Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain or dyspnea on exertion.
• Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath
• Musculoskeletal: Bilateral ankle swelling.
• Psychiatric: Endorses depression.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Neurological/Psychological: Appropriate mood and affect.
• Respiratory: Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
• Cardiovascular: Slight 3/6 systolic ejection murmur, stable.
• Musculoskeletal: 1-2+ pitting edema in the bilateral lower extremities.

VITALS REVIEWED

• Blood Pressure: Elevated at 156/94 mmHg.

RESULTS

Labs: Creatinine is within normal limits.
Electrocardiogram is normal. No evidence of coronary artery disease.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Edward Butler is a 59-year-old male with a past medical history significant for depression, hypertension, and prior rotator cuff repair. He presents for a follow-up of his chronic problems.

Depression.
• Medical Reasoning: He is doing well with therapy and physical activity. He continues to decline medication for this.
• Medical Treatment: Continue current management strategies.
• Patient Education and Counseling: I encouraged him to contact me should he want to try medication.

Hypertension.
• Medical Reasoning: This is uncontrolled at this time. He is compliant with Norvasc, but admits to occasional dietary indiscretion.
• Additional Testing: We will order a lipid panel and an echocardiogram.
• Medical Treatment: Increase Norvasc to 10 mg daily.

8 months status post rotator cuff repair.
• Medical Reasoning: He is doing well postoperatively. He has progressed from formal physical therapy to solely home exercise.
• Medical Treatment: Continue with home exercise.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's blood pressure readings have been running high over the past couple of months.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This describes the recent course of home blood pressure readings over time, which is part of the patient's history/chronology rather than an in-clinic exam or test result. However, it does not substantially mislead the reader since it is still a clinical observation about blood pressure control.
